title: Where the Distance Formula Comes From

md: The distance between two points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ has a formula that looks intimidating:
But there is nothing new to learn here. This formula is the **Pythagorean theorem** ($a^2 + b^2 = c^2$) with coordinates plugged in.

blocks.title: The claim
blocks.body: The straight-line distance between $(x_1,y_1)$ and $(x_2,y_2)$ is $\;d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2+(y_2-y_1)^2}.$

md: ### Step 1: Hide a right triangle in the picture

Drag the two points below and turn on the rise/run triangle. From any two points, drop one **horizontal** segment and one **vertical** segment. They always meet at a right angle, and the line joining your two points is the **hypotenuse**.
How long is the **horizontal** leg? It only depends on how far apart the $x$-coordinates are:
And the **vertical** leg depends only on the $y$-coordinates:
The distance $d$ is the hypotenuse. Pythagoras says hypotenuse² = leg² + leg²:
Take the square root of both sides, and since a distance can't be negative, we keep the positive root:

blocks.title: Why the order of subtraction doesn't matter
blocks.body: What if you compute $x_1 - x_2$ instead of $x_2 - x_1$? You'd get the opposite sign, but the formula **squares** each difference, and squaring erases the sign. $(3-7)^2 = (-4)^2 = 16 = (7-3)^2$. The leg lengths come out right either way.
